passages.explanation: When morning comes, all the chief priests and elders take counsel against Jesus to put him to death. They bind him, lead him away, and deliver him to Pontius Pilate, the governor. Then Judas, who betrayed him, seeing that Jesus is condemned, feels remorse. He brings back the thirty pieces of silver to the chief priests and elders: I have sinned. I betrayed innocent blood. They say: what is that to us? You see to it yourself. He throws the silver into the sanctuary, departs, and hangs himself. The chief priests take the silver coins — it's not lawful to put them into the treasury since it's the price of blood. They buy the potter's field as a burial place for strangers. Matthew sees this fulfilling Jeremiah's prophecy about thirty pieces of silver and the potter's field. Judas' remorse is real, but he goes to the wrong people for absolution. The priests who paid for the betrayal refuse responsibility for it.

Jesus stands before the governor. Pilate asks: are you the King of the Jews? Jesus answers: you say so. When accused by the chief priests and elders, he gives no answer. Pilate asks: don't you hear all the testimony against you? Jesus doesn't answer even one word, and the governor marvels greatly. At the feast, the governor customarily releases one prisoner to the crowd. They have a notorious prisoner called Barabbas. Pilate asks: whom do you want me to release — Barabbas or Jesus who is called Christ? He knows they delivered Jesus out of envy. While he sits on the judgement seat, his wife sends a message: have nothing to do with that righteous man — I've suffered many things in a dream today because of him. But the chief priests and elders persuade the multitudes to ask for Barabbas and destroy Jesus. Pilate asks: which one? They say Barabbas. What then shall I do with Jesus? They all say: let him be crucified! Pilate asks: what evil has he done? They shout louder: let him be crucified! Pilate takes water and washes his hands before the crowd: I'm innocent of this righteous man's blood. You see to it. All the people answer: his blood be on us and on our children! He releases Barabbas, has Jesus flogged, and delivers him to be crucified. The crowd chooses a criminal over the Christ. Pilate tries to shift responsibility with water. The mob takes it on with words they don't understand the weight of.

The governor's soldiers take Jesus into the Praetorium and gather the whole garrison around him. They strip him and put a scarlet robe on him. They weave a crown of thorns and put it on his head, with a reed in his right hand. They kneel before him and mock him: Hail, King of the Jews! They spit on him, take the reed, and strike him on the head. When they finish mocking, they take the robe off, put his own clothes back on, and lead him away to be crucified. The mockery is brutal — but every element is accidentally true. The robe, the crown, the sceptre, the kneeling. They're dressing up the actual King of the Jews and don't know it.

As they go out, they find a man of Cyrene named Simon and compel him to carry Jesus' cross. They come to Golgotha — the Place of a Skull. They offer him wine mixed with gall; he tastes it but won't drink. They crucify him and divide his garments by casting lots. They sit and keep watch. Above his head they put the charge: this is Jesus, the King of the Jews. Two robbers are crucified with him, one on the right and one on the left. Those passing by revile him, wagging their heads: you who destroy the temple and build it in three days, save yourself! If you are the Son of God, come down from the cross! The chief priests, scribes, and elders mock likewise: he saved others but he can't save himself. If he's the King of Israel, let him come down now and we'll believe. He trusts in God — let God deliver him if he wants him. He said, I am the Son of God. Even the robbers revile him. The irony is layered: they mock him for not saving himself, not realising that saving himself would mean not saving them. Coming down from the cross would prove he could — but staying on it proves he would.

From the sixth hour there is darkness over all the land until the ninth hour — noon to three in the afternoon. About the ninth hour, Jesus cries out with a loud voice: Eli, Eli, lama sabachthani? — My God, my God, why have you forsaken me? Some standing there say: this man is calling Elijah. One runs, takes a sponge, fills it with vinegar, puts it on a reed, and gives him a drink. The rest say: leave him alone — let's see whether Elijah comes to save him. Jesus cries out again with a loud voice and yields up his spirit. The cry of dereliction is Psalm 22:1 — not despair but the experience of bearing the full weight of human sin and separation. Three hours of darkness and then this: the Son feels the absence of the Father.

The veil of the temple is torn in two from top to bottom. The earth quakes. The rocks are split. The tombs are opened, and many bodies of the saints who had fallen asleep are raised. They come out of the tombs after his resurrection, go into the holy city, and appear to many. The centurion and those watching Jesus, seeing the earthquake and the things that are done, are terrified: truly this was the Son of God! Many women are there watching from a distance — among them Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James and Joses, and the mother of Zebedee's sons. The veil tore from top to bottom — from God's side down. The barrier between God and humanity is removed. A Roman centurion makes the confession that Israel's leaders refused to make.

When evening comes, a rich man from Arimathea named Joseph — who himself is also Jesus' disciple — goes to Pilate and asks for Jesus' body. Pilate commands it to be given to him. Joseph takes the body, wraps it in a clean linen cloth, and lays it in his own new tomb which he has cut out of the rock. He rolls a great stone against the door of the tomb and departs. Mary Magdalene and the other Mary are there, sitting opposite the tomb. A secret disciple comes out of the shadows at the moment of greatest risk. Joseph provides a rich man's tomb for the one who had nowhere to lay his head.

The next day — the day after the Preparation — the chief priests and Pharisees gather before Pilate. They say: sir, we remember that deceiver said while he was still alive, after three days I will rise again. Command the tomb to be made secure until the third day, or his disciples may come, steal him, and tell the people he is risen from the dead — the last deception would be worse than the first. Pilate tells them: you have a guard. Go, make it as secure as you can. They seal the stone and set a guard. The irony is profound: the religious leaders remember Jesus' resurrection prediction better than the disciples do. Their precautions will become the very evidence that the resurrection wasn't a theft.
